text:
I had a brilliant time with @morgan_downey as his very first guest on The Commodities Show.

From my origins to how China's move to coal-to-olefins pushed naphtha into mogas, to the Golden Era of Refining (or not)... Great stuff to share! 

YouTube link in comments.

#oott

quotedTweet.text:
"Did chinese plastics switching to ethane just save the global economy from an oil price spike" 

I have launched a new interview show focused on the structural forces underlying commodity markets, and I wanted to share the first episode with you.

Episode 1: June Goh on the Physical Layer Above the Crude Price

Full Youtube video in comments

text: @morgan_downey Here to watch https://t.co/uU7OsWfQLl

CHART OF THE DAY: The hottest commodity? 

It isn't oil or fertiliser. The commodity in question is weathering an even greater supply shock and record prices: The anchovy — and its derivative, fishmeal (key for global aquaculture).

My @Opinion column: https://t.co/f7q7NRui58 https://t.co/zwcqxXMaPC

U.S. Oil Recovery Threatened by Lower Prices

U.S. oil production accelerated to a record rate after the war with Iran and closure of the Strait of Hormuz boosted prices and dispelled some of the gloom hanging over the industry at the end of 2025.

Total production of crude and condensates accelerated to 13.9 million barrels per day in April, surpassing the previous record set six months earlier in October 2025.

In the short term, faster completions, unchoking existing wells and more production from stripper wells have all probably boosted output, but a more sustained increase requires more drilling ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to clients earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

GLOBAL DATA CENTRES consumed almost 780 billion kilowatt-hours of electricity in 2025 up from 410 billion in 2020. Consumption has increased by an average of almost 14% per year. As a result, data centre consumption was equivalent to about 2.5% of global generation up from 1.5% five years earlier:
https://t.co/CAQxjgiIU9

quotedTweet.text: Just a glimpse of the madness to come. https://t.co/gWsIu0V6JS

text:
Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours.

Visible escorts happening under US protection. There were 4 VLCCs that went in with AIS on. 

On the Iranian side, inbound tankers remain limited ex-Iran.

So far, no escalation has occurred from IRGC. I was wrong to assume that the $6 billion had already been returned to the Iranians. 

I guess time will tell what's really going on. 

Video from @MarineTraffic and I have included the table of inbound tankers below. VLCCs not related to Iran are highlighted.

At this point, there is no economic reason for China not to be buying crude hand over fist. 

None, whatsoever. https://t.co/XjKNQo5C7u
🇨🇦 Happy Canada Day 🇨🇦 https://t.co/5dxTsTOvA6
Iranian state TV cuts short interview with Iran's top negotiator, drawing ire from his team https://t.co/u0jmHlzH2Q
Ship Iran claimed was stranded in strait has been moored in Iranian waters since March https://t.co/xGuuVIuxeE

Ahh, so it's THIS vessel. This is ARISTA (9348493). She is Comoros-flagged (as far as being "foreign" goes) but is part of Iran's Shamkhani network. ARISTA has been under US OFAC sanctions since last summer. She's actually been stuck in this same spot since mid-March 2026; at 27.12845, 56.46221; north of Hormuz Island, Iran.

quotedTweet.text: A cargo ship attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz using a US-suggested route has run aground; an incident even worse than sinking! https://t.co/KvUvUIkMXI

text: Here's ARISTA (9348493) on today's satellite imagery. https://t.co/LiLgrgZgR5
It'll be interesting to learn how a vessel with an AIS-reported draft depth of 10 meters ran aground in 35-50 meters of water.

quotedTweet.text: Iranian state media IRIB claims a foreign ship has run aground in the Strait of Hormuz after attempting to take a route other than the Iranian-approved route. It adds that "proper depth and length for the passage of a variety of wide-body ships, especially oil tankers, is located only in the territorial waters of our beloved country of Iran." Haven't seen any confirmation of their claim about the ship.

text:
We’re starting to see a clear, gradual ramp-up in Persian Gulf loading activity.

Looking at the optical satellite imagery, berths that were sitting completely empty even after the MoU was inked are finally seeing hulls tied up alongside.

Unsurprisingly, Iran and the UAE are leading the charge here. I’m expecting the Saudi, Kuwaiti, and Iraqi books to start showing a gradual recovery soon too.

The satellite birds caught the Iranian and Saudi prints on June 30th, and the UAE tape on July 1st.

#oott #iran
DECLARATION OF EMERGENCY AND AUTHORIZATION FOR TEMPORARY DUTY FREE IMPORTATION OF PHOSPHATE FERTILIZER FROM MOROCCO - US State Department

Javier Milei: There is an alliance between the radical left and Islamist terrorism. This is more than a temporary alliance. It is an evil brotherhood founded on hatred for Western civilization https://t.co/503DWQvdMh
THE EIA EXPECTS GLOBAL OIL SUPPLY TO OUTPACE DEMAND THROUGH 2027, FORECASTING HIGHER INVENTORIES AND LOWER PRICES, WITH BRENT AVERAGING ABOUT $57.69/BBL IN 2026 AND AROUND $53/BBL IN 2027.

7.1.26

GEOPOLITICS, MACROECONOMICS & ENERGY
- US-Iran Diplomacy: President Trump weighed a return to all-out war but opted to stick with diplomatic talks following discussions with defense officials. US negotiators report positive technical discussions in Qatar. 
- Oil Glut Warning: Brent crude extended its decline below $72/bbl. Goldman Sachs warns the crude market will swing back into a massive oversupply, projecting a surplus of over 3 million bpd next year. API reported a 6 million barrel crude draw ahead of today's DOE data.
- USMCA Sunset: The Trump administration is expected to formally declare on Wednesday that it will not extend the USMCA trade agreement, triggering a "sunset clause" that starts a decade-long clock to wind down the North American free trade zone.
- Historic Yen Weakness: USD/JPY hit a fresh 40-year high of 162.82 with no immediate signs of BOJ intervention. Analysts note there are no major technical chart levels until the Plaza Accord era of 240.00 yen.
- UK Political Transition: The British pound suffered its weakest start to a year since 2022. Investors are on edge as Labour Prime Minister Keir Starmer steps down, shifting focus to how likely replacement Andy Burnham will manage the UK economy and stretched finances.

FEDERAL RESERVE & CENTRAL BANKS
- Sintra Symposium: Fed Chair Kevin Warsh speaks on a highly anticipated panel at 9:00 a.m. ET at the ECB's Sintra conference, alongside Lagarde, Bailey, and Macklem. Traders are scrutinizing Warsh for rate path clues after his strict price-stability pledge at the June FOMC.
- Rate Hike Odds: Futures markets currently price a 33% chance of a Fed rate hike later this month, and a 67% to 88% probability of a hike by September.
- ECB Outlook: Eurozone inflation eased significantly last month, drastically reducing pressure on the European Central Bank. Markets currently price just a 5% chance of an ECB rate hike on July 17.

EQUITIES, TECH & CORPORATE M&A
- Q2 Tech Performance & NVDA: The S&P 500 (+15%) and Nasdaq (+21%) just posted their best quarters since 2020. However, under the hood of the record-breaking SOX index, Nvidia (NVDA) was stunningly the worst single-performing stock in the benchmark for the quarter.
- Mega-M&A Record: Driven by $10B+ mega-deals, global M&A hit a record $2.8 trillion in the first half of 2026 (up 48% YoY), despite the total number of announced deals falling to a six-year low.
- Earnings & AI Growth: Consensus expects S&P 500 EPS to grow 22% YoY in the upcoming earnings season. AI infrastructure companies are projected to drive 60% of that growth, expanding earnings at triple the rate of the broader index.
- AI Job Cuts: The financial and information sectors are seeing accelerating job losses (averaging 28,000/month in 2026) as tech companies increasingly cite their heavy AI investments as the catalyst for workforce reductions.
- South Korea Pension Fears: NPS Chairman Kim Sung-joo dismissed fears of a 74 trillion won "selling bomb" in domestic equities, stating the chance of disruptive selling is "zero" even as a temporary waiver on domestic equity rebalancing expires.

DOMESTIC POLITICS
- Progressive Upsets: Democratic socialists scored major primary upsets in Colorado, including Melat Kiros unseating 29-year incumbent Diana DeGette. Combined with recent wins in NY and Maine, progressive momentum is disrupting the establishment status quo.
- Student Loan Rollout: New Department of Education repayment plans, implemented under the Trump administration, go into effect on July 1. Featuring stricter borrowing caps, the changes are expected to trigger "massive sticker shock" for millions of borrowers transitioning to new plans this summer.

CALENDAR
- Wed: Canada Day (Canadian Markets Closed). China/Japan/EU/UK Manufacturing PMIs. US ADP Employment (8:15 a.m. ET), ISM Manufacturing, MBA Mortgages. Sintra Central Bank Panel (Warsh, Lagarde, Bailey, Macklem at 9:00 a.m. ET). DOE Oil Inventories (10:30 a.m. ET).
- Thu: US June Nonfarm Payrolls (8:30 a.m. ET - moved up for Friday holiday), Weekly Jobless Claims, Durable Goods, Factory Orders. Japan 10-Year Bond Auction. EU Unemployment. US Credit Markets Early Close (2:00 p.m. ET).
- Fri: US Markets Closed (Independence Day Observed). China/Aussie/EU PMIs. BOE's Bailey Speaks. CFTC COT, Fed H8 Report.

ICYMI O/N

MACRO:

The Trump administration and Anthropic have reached an agreement to restore access to the company’s most recent general-access AI model. Anthropic said it would begin restoring access Wednesday after a 2½-week shutdown, saying it had implemented a new safeguard to address the behavior Amazon researchers flagged after working closely with the government. (WSJ)

US President Trump’s forays into cryptocurrency delivered him a windfall of more than $1 billion last year, according to his latest financial disclosure report, an unprecedented surge in income that came alongside earnings from royalty deals, real estate and legal settlements. (WSJ)

Pierre Yared, the acting head of the White House Council of Economic Advisers, is stepping down, Trump said Tuesday, as the president’s nominee to hold the role permanently nears likely confirmation by the Senate. (WSJ)

Global equity fundraising topped $729.4 billion in the first half of 2026, boosted by SpaceX's record initial public offering, and marking the second-best start to a year on record, according to Mergermarket data. (Dow Jones)

Elon Musk said in an X post, pushing back on a view an AI crash is coming, "There are always momentary dips, even in a rapidly growing economy. The productivity gains from AI and robotics are so enormous, however, that the macro trend is overwhelmingly up." (X)

Fossil fuel power investment in the US is set to outpace China for the first time in decades thanks to a surge in gas-fired turbine orders, as companies rush to build out data centres. (FT)

Despite President Trump’s defeat at the Supreme Court in his bid to limit birthright citizenship, he has notched several recent court victories that are expected to turbocharge his deportation efforts. (WSJ)

The global oil market is set to swing back into oversupply as the impact of the Iran war fades and traffic through the Strait of Hormuz recovers, according to Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (BBG)

The global AI boom powered Asia's manufacturing sector in June, with brisk demand for technology-related goods offsetting the drag from the Iran war, business surveys showed on Wednesday, offering some relief for the region's export-reliant economies. (RTRS)

Japan’s currency chief suggested intervention was an effective strategy and flagged close communication between Tokyo and Washington over foreign exchange, in comments made as the yen hovers near a four-decade low. (BBG)

The slump in the yen is forcing investors to rethink their strategy to gauge the currency's moves, according to Mizuho Bank They note the relationship between interest-rate differentials and the dollar-yen level has reversed, with the yen weakening even as domestic yields have climbed. (BBG)

Japanese PM Takaichi set a target for raising Japan’s nominal GDP to ¥1,100 trillion ($6.8 trillion) alongside annual domestic private-sector capital investment of ¥230 trillion by the fiscal year starting in April 2040, as she champions her vision for a strong economy. (BBG)

Japan's manufacturing sector expanded in June, sealing its best quarterly performance since early 2014, as new orders grew at their fastest pace in more than two years, a business survey showed on Wednesday. (RTRS)

Japanese business mood climbed to an eight-year high and corporate inflation expectations rose to record levels, a quarterly survey showed, underlining the economy's resilience to the Middle East war and bolstering the case for more rate hikes. (RTRS)

A private gauge (RatingDog) showed China’s manufacturing activity grew at a slower pace in June due to tapered factory production, diverging from a pickup shown in a competing index. (WSJ)

Bullish bets on the Chinese yuan are fading, with options traders unwinding one of the year’s most crowded trades after the Federal Reserve’s hawkish pivot. (BBG)

China’s A-share trading volume set a record high for a first half with semiconductor valuations eclipsing major state-owned banks for the first time ever as AI demand drew investors. (Securities Times)

Chinese carmakers are rushing to cut their dependence on foreign chips in an echo of how their dominance of battery technology gave them an upper hand in making electric vehicles. (FT)

The war in Iran has allowed China's petrochemicals trade to boost exports and ease its glut of key building blocks for plastics, rubber, and textiles. Buyers from Vietnam to Indonesia have tapped China for supply due to the loss of feedstock from the Persian Gulf, and Chinese producers have established supply chains to the rest of Asia. (BBG)

South Korea’s exports posted the strongest growth in nearly 50 years as the global artificial-intelligence buildout keeps fueling demand for chips. (WSJ)

South Korea’s won slid toward its weakest level since the global financial crisis, leading a retreat in Asian currencies, as the dollar strengthened and overseas investors sold local equities. (BBG)

British annual house prices rose by less than expected in June, but softening expectations for interest rate hikes by the Bank of England are likely to improve affordability, mortgage lender Nationwide said on Wednesday. (Guardian)

The EU will limit the amount of tariff-free steel its member states can import from Wednesday, as part of officials’ efforts to protect its steel industry from oversupply from countries like China. (WSJ)

The ECB should not rush into any further rate hike given the unexpectedly quick retreat in oil prices, Maltese central bank chief Alexander Demarco said, joining a growing chorus of policymakers calling for caution. (RTRS)

Germany is pressing the US to allow more American weapons to be built on German soil, seeking to plug Europe’s military gaps while giving Donald Trump a reason to remain invested in the continent’s defence. (FT)

Just 12 per cent of crypto companies that have been operating in the EU are approved to continue doing so from Wednesday under tough new rules that are shrinking the industry and forcing many of them to wind down their businesses. (FT)

India's manufacturing sector expanded at its second-slowest pace in four years in June as cooling demand for goods dragged on output and hiring but easing cost pressures provided some relief, a survey showed. (RTRS)

India’s bonds open to unrestricted global investment attracted a record monthly inflow after the government scrapped taxes on foreign investors. (BBG)

Malaysia’s spending on petrol and diesel subsidies could climb to 40 billion ringgit ($9.8 billion) in 2026 if current market prices persist, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said. The projected cost would be more than double the 15 billion ringgit allocated for subsidies in this year’s budget. (BBG)

GEOPOLITICAL:

'A Dispute Over Opening Hormuz Drives a Wedge Into U.S.-Saudi Relations' WSJ article notes a diplomatic rift has emerged since Saudi blocked U.S. military access for Project Freedom. The U.S. is considering reducing its military footprint in Saudi Arabia, focusing forces on more supportive countries like Israel and Jordan. (WSJ)

'Putin Faces a Political Crisis as Fuel Shortages Ripple Through Russia' WSJ article notes Ukrainian drone attacks have caused widespread fuel shortages across Russia, which has led to Russia planning to import fuel for the first time in decades and has banned gasoline and jet fuel exports. Comes ahead of September parliamentary elections. (WSJ)

China's covert military training of Russian forces last year was personally approved by President Vladimir Putin's defence minister and directly involved at least four Russian and Chinese generals, according to two European officials and documents seen by Reuters. (RTRS)

EQUITIES:

SoftBank Group has executed the $10b follow-on investment in OpenAI that was previously announced in March. Plans to complete the remaining $10b investment on Oct. 1. (BBG)

Bloom Energy and Brookfield have expanded their partnership to ‌finance power projects for AI infrastructure, boosting their funding framework fivefold to $25 billion to accelerate the global deployment of Bloom's ​fuel cells. Shares of Bloom Energy surged 12% in ​extended trading. (RTRS)

Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra said Tuesday in a CNBC interview that memory chipmakers aren’t the only ones to blame for the current supply-and-demand imbalance. Customers who drove a hard bargain in pricing in recent years also contributed to the squeeze, Mehrotra argued, suggesting that left the industry underinvested for the AI boom. (CNBC)

Nike recorded another sales decline in the latest quarter as it continued to hit hurdles with weakness in China. The company’s shares dropped more than 7% after the market close. (WSJ)

Microsoft is planning to cut under 2.5% of its workforce in the latest round of layoffs that could be announced as early as next week, according to Business Insider sources. (BI)

Constellation Brands said higher gas prices and economic uncertainty dampened sales of its alcoholic beverages after a promising start in its most recent quarter. (WSJ)

The Trump administration is drafting a ban on imports of foreign inverters, which connect solar projects and batteries to the grid, over concerns China could use them to disrupt power supplies, according to Reuters sources. (RTRS)

KKR said it agreed to acquire the U.S. and Canadian operations of EDF power solutions from EDF group for about $4.2 billion, with potential additional payments of up to $390 million. (WSJ)

KKR and South Korea’s SK Inc. are jointly launching a $1.3 billion renewable-energy platform aimed at meeting surging demand for clean power in AI data centers and semiconductor manufacturing. (WSJ)

South Korea's antitrust regulator alleged on Wednesday that Alphabet's Google abused its dominant position in the Android app marketplace to hinder competition and will recommend corrective measures and a financial penalty. (RTRS)

European Technology Commissioner Henna Virkkunen had a "constructive" talk with Apple's Chief Executive Tim Cook during a video call held on Monday, said a Commission spokesperson. (RTRS)

French container shipping group CMA CGM is nearing a deal to buy FedEx's third-party logistics business for $1.4 billion in cash, according to FT sources. (FT)

US data center equipment maker Vertiv Holdings Co. opened a factory in Malaysia, underscoring the rapid pace of AI infrastructure buildout in the Asia Pacific. (BBG)

Lime, backed by Uber, raised $167 million in its U.S. initial public offering, joining a growing list of companies tapping a revived market for new listings. (RTRS)

Dish DBS, the satellite pay-TV provider under Charlie Ergen’s EchoStar broadcast empire, filed for bankruptcy Tuesday after a deal to sell spectrum to AT&T was held up. (WSJ) 

Chinese chip material manufacturers are boosting output of cutting-edge products, such as T-glass, aiming to catch longtime Japanese market leaders as Beijing pushes semiconductor self-sufficiency. (Nikkei)

TikTok is considering cutting about 300 jobs at its European hub in Dublin, following a similarly sized round of layoffs last year. (BBG)

Ipsen said it agreed to buy Swiss biotechnology company Memo Therapeutics in a deal that could exceed 700 million euros ($799.6 million) and that seeks to expand the French drugmaker’s rare-disease portfolio. (WSJ)

Primark owner Associated British Foods said on Wednesday it still expected annual profit to come in below last year's level, with its sugar business loss-making amid a further deterioration in trading conditions. (RTRS)

Britain's biggest fast-food chain Greggs said on Wednesday that its CFO Richard Hutton will step down after 28 years with the company. (RTRS)

Alcoa has agreed to buy South32’s bauxite, alumina and aluminum assets across Australia, Brazil and South Africa in a cash-and-stock deal valued at up to $5.6 billion. (WSJ)

总体市场评分：5.5/10（方向性情绪：+1）

主要偏多因素：
- 美联储主席Warsh表示近期通胀风险已缓和，强化了对回归2%目标的信心，美国经济韧性强，短期美债收益率回落，支持宽松预期。
- 原油价格显著回落，WTI跌1.32%至68.58美元，布伦特跌1.89%至71.57美元，因伊朗谈判进展及霍尔木兹海峡运输恢复，市场从紧俏转向潜在过剩定价，降低能源成本利好企业盈利与消费。
- ADP数据显示6月美国私营部门新增就业9.8万，市场聚焦周四非农就业报告对美联储利率路径的影响。
- 苹果与特朗普政府就芯片采购进行谈判，同时探讨从中国厂商采购内存芯片，显示供应链多元化努力。
- Robinhood推出加密交易产品及Robinhood Chain L2区块链，加密相关业务扩张带来风险偏好提升信号。

主要偏空因素：
- 盘中MOC失衡显示道指与MAG7出现卖盘，标普与纳指虽正但芯片股走弱抵消大盘涨幅。
- 油价回落虽利好增长，但反映全球供应过剩担忧，高盛与摩根士丹利均下调油价预测，地缘政治风险仍存。
- 美国拒绝以现形式续签USMCA，启动未来十年年度审查并可能重谈加墨贸易协定，关税与原产地规则分歧或增加企业不确定性。
- 黄金上涨伴随美元走强，投资者重新评估美联储政策路径，显示避险情绪未完全消退。
- 伊朗议会发言人限制核查员访问权限，地缘紧张及委内瑞拉政治动态可能扰动市场情绪。

相比上次更新的变化：
- 新增苹果芯片采购谈判及中国内存采购讨论，成为市场关注焦点。
- 美联储Warsh表态强化宽松信心，油价加速回落为新驱动。

text:
#CoinQFlash ⚡

📉#Bitcoin touched a 21-month low near $57,950 today following a record $4.51 billion in June outflows from U.S. spot ETFs, driven largely by selling pressure from BlackRock's iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT).

🛢️Brent crude is hovering near $70 a barrel after posting its steepest quarterly decline since 2020, as global markets closely monitor the ongoing US-Iran ceasefire negotiations in Qatar.

📈The #SP500 opened July trading near the 7,490 level after notching a massive 14.87% second-quarter gain, with investors now bracing for today's June ADP employment and ISM manufacturing data drops.

Nifty 24,000 Reclaimed: Gap-Up Loading! 🚀

​The bears just lost their grip! As seen in 185210.jpg, GIFT Nifty is surging +142.50 points (+0.59%) to 24,186.50, signaling a strong gap-up start for D-Street today! 📈🟢

​Today's Market Pulse:
💥 GST Power: June collections hit a massive ₹1.95 Lakh Crore (+13.9% YoY)—proving domestic demand is rock solid! 💪

🛢️ Crude Cools: Brent crude slips near $72/bbl as Doha talks advance, boosting margins for Auto & Chemical sectors. ✨

⚠️ IT Strategy: Nasdaq's overnight tech sell-off means Nifty IT remains under pressure. Don't chase the opening IT gap; focus on relative strength elsewhere!

​Expiry Game Plan: 24,000 is now the crucial psychological floor. Let the morning volatility settle before committing capital! 🎯

​#WealthWisdomWave #Nifty50 #StockMarketIndia #Trading

🔴 $QQQ $725.17 (-1.52%) (close)
Brent crude posted its worst quarter since 2020 as easing Middle East tensions weighed on prices, with WTI down 1.09% today at $67.83 after talks between Washington and Tehran concluded. The diplomatic progress reduced the geopolitical risk premium that had been supporting energy markets, adding further pressure to an already soft quarter for oil.

*Stuck in a Range*

*Global Cues*

- *US-Iran deal on track*: Going *as per schedule*, but *US markets closed mildly lower* — Nasdaq corrected from highs
- *Asia weak*: Most *Asian markets trading lower* today
- *Theme shift*: The *trade that led for last 3-4 months now consolidating* → Watch for rotation
- *Commodities*: *Gold & silver bounced sharply* from lows but *stuck near support*, no momentum yet. *Brent crude fell to ∼$70/bbl* → Back to *pre-war levels*, big positive for India
- *Overall*: *Global cues slightly negative*

*Indian Markets*

- *Good buying yesterday*: Nifty saw *strong buying, closed near 24,000* led by banks
- *Stuck in range*: *23,800 = key support*, holding for long. But *24,200 resistance not breached* → Nifty range-bound
- *Breakout level*: *24,200 on closing basis = momentum trigger*. Below that, chop continues

*Strategy*

- *Index view*: *Bullish as long as 23,800 holds*. Stay long above it
- *Momentum call*: *Close above 24,200 needed* for fresh impetus to market
- *Sector picks*: *Bank Nifty leading*. *Avoid IT* — stocks near *5-year lows*
- *Preferred*: *Banks, NBFCs, select Defence* — continue to be bullish there
- *Playbook*: *Forget Nifty, focus on stocks*. Trade with *proper hedging in place* and *ride the momentum*
, The current WTI Crude price (~$67.83–$68) is now lower than (or back to) levels just before the recent Iran-related war/escalation began

$NIFTY PREMARKET INTEL — 07/02/26
🤖 AI / IT / SEMICONDUCTORS
• Global Sentiment — US tech equities finished June with massive quarterly gains; the robust structural tone provides a supportive backdrop for domestic IT counters to recover from recent distribution.
• Sector Rebound — Early indications suggest tech heavyweights may attract short-covering capital as institutional risk appetite aggressively returns for the start of the new financial half.
📈 UPGRADES / ANALYST
• Nifty 50 PA — Reclaimed the psychological 24,000 threshold, advancing +0.59% to close at 24,005.85; sustained PA above this level targets the 24,200 liquidity pool.
• Bank Nifty PA — Exploded +490.15 points (+0.85%) to decisively clear resistance at 58,033.05; structural momentum confirms a bullish continuation if the 58,000 zone converts to firm support.
⚠️ MACRO / RBI / ECONOMIC DATA
• Global Cues — US markets closed June on a remarkably strong note with the Nasdaq climbing +1.52% to 26,213 and the S&P 500 reaching 7,499; this risk-on surge injects significant structural optimism into the Asian open.
• Derivative Flows — Options data reflects a dominant shift in institutional positioning, with heavy put writing at the 23,800 base aggressively trapping late-arriving bears from earlier in the week.
🛢️ OIL & GAS / COMMODITIES
• Brent Crude — Stabilized following recent volatility; the fundamental lack of a geopolitical risk premium keeps structural energy thresholds firmly secure for domestic oil marketing companies.
• Sector Rotation — High-conviction buying remains visible in FMCG and Auto counters; easing margin pressures support long-term accumulation across domestic consumer plays.
🚀 IPO / DEALS
• Primary Market — Dalal Street enters the second half of the calendar year with a stacked IPO pipeline; institutional liquidity remains robust enough to absorb upcoming mega-listings without draining secondary market momentum.
💊 BIOTECH / PHARMA
• Defensive Anchor — $SUNPHARMA continues to exhibit sustained relative strength, acting as a primary defensive hedge while high-beta sectors command the broader risk-on rally.
🌍 GEOPOLITICAL
• Risk Premium — The stabilization of Middle East transit routes has completely neutralized immediate supply chain anxieties, allowing markets to shift focus entirely back to Q1 corporate earnings and global rate trajectories.
📦 OTHER
• Pre-Market Setup — GIFT Nifty is trading with aggressive strength near 24,159, commanding a premium of over 150 points to the cash close and signaling a massive gap-up open for Thursday's session.
• Stock Specifics — $TITAN and $KOTAKBANK have triggered fresh PA breakout signals; both counters are positioned to lead their respective sectors amidst expanding institutional credit growth.
• MindsetMarkets PA Outlook — Strictly follow the PA structural model and ignore 15-minute chart noise. Wait for clean liquidity sweeps at established order blocks, cap total portfolio risk at 20%, and limit individual stock exposure to a strict maximum of 10%.

Middle East: Diplomacy Meets Oil

Geopolitical headlines remained a mixed bag. Qatar reported “positive progress” in indirect US-Iran talks, although Tehran confirmed no direct negotiations took place. Both sides agreed to continue discussions after Iran’s mourning period, keeping hopes of diplomacy alive. That optimism, combined with rising crude shipments through the Strait of Hormuz and stronger exports from Iran, the UAE and Russia, pushed Brent crude toward its lowest level since February. Meanwhile, the region reminded everyone it never sleeps, with fresh Israeli strikes in Gaza and Lebanon keeping geopolitical risks firmly on the radar.

On January 28th, 2026 West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil closed $63.21 per barrel across U.S. markets when gold reached $5,597.23 inverting the curves. The inversion is being inverted now as the price of WTI reaches 67.83. https://t.co/A36Ucp5Nbe

With the yields on USTs skyrocketing, gold is the de facto safe haven, the condicio sine qua non. https://t.co/MckxI7rYK9

​Following intense military pressure involving three consecutive nights of heavy strikes, U.S. President Donald Trump announced that diplomatic engagements regarding the denuclearization of Iran are progressing favorably.

Trump highlighted that recent high-level meetings have yielded constructive dialogue, signaling an unexpected turnaround.

Despite the prior escalation, he maintained that bilateral communications are currently moving along well as both nations navigate nuclear negotiations.

Iran has exported 50 million barrels in the past two weeks, earning an estimated $3.5 billion.

Tehran plans to impose transit fees after the 60-day free-passage period ends in mid-August; the U.S. has rejected the proposal.

The recovery signals easing supply risks and reduced leverage over the key global oil chokepoint.

Daily Commodities Wrap-Up: July 1, 2026

Grains

The agricultural pits carried intense, broad-based buying momentum into Wednesday’s session, capitalizing fully on the dramatic structural reset triggered by Tuesday’s landmark USDA reports. Corn futures extended their post-report short squeeze, surging more than 2% to cross back over the $4.22 threshold as the market continued to digest the severe 120-million-bushel deficit in June 1 quarterly stockpiles. 

Wheat futures kept pace, marching higher as the electronic desks aggressively priced in the government’s deeper-than-expected cuts to all-wheat and spring wheat planted footprint. Soybeans also enjoyed steady, demand-driven buying, shaking off earlier acreage weight to participate in the macro ag liftoff. High-resolution weather models turning hot and dry across the northern I-states corridor over the next two weeks provided a strong secondary fundamental spark, amplifying automated buying.

Corn (September '26) - Open: $4.18 1/2, Close: $4.22 1/4, +$0.03 3/4, Trend: Strongly Bullish, Support: $4.15, Resistance: $4.28

Soybeans (August '26) - Open: $11.25 1/2, Close: $11.38, +$0.12 1/2, Trend: Bullish Tilt, Support: $11.20, Resistance: $11.50

Wheat (Chicago September '26) - Open: $5.88 1/2, Close: $5.98 1/2, +$0.10, Trend: Bullish, Support: $5.85, Resistance: $6.08

Market Note: With corn silking at 9% three percentage points ahead of the historical five-year average the critical pollination window has officially opened earlier than usual. This accelerated timeline means any localized high-pressure heat dome will land on an ag room that is highly sensitive to yield adjustments following the surprise drain in old-crop stocks.

Livestock

The cattle complex experienced a day of choppy, defensive consolidation on Wednesday, giving back a portion of yesterday's post-report gains to light speculative profit-taking. Traders stepped back to monitor initial mid-week cash trade indicators after early-morning country packer bids of $252.00 to $255.00 live were passed on with authority by feedlot managers. 

The underlying structural framework remains incredibly firm; northern show lists are noticeably smaller, and wholesale beef prices have maintained a strong posture near the key $400 mark. The long-term supply squeeze underscored by a year-to-date slaughter pace trailing nearly 9% behind last year’s clip continues to provide feedlots with ultimate leverage during negotiations.

Live Cattle (August '26) - Open: $244.80, Close: $243.85, -$0.95, Trend: Bullish (Consolidating), Support: $241.50, Resistance: $246.50

Feeder Cattle (August '26) - Open: $369.95, Close: $366.65, -$3.30, Trend: Strongly Bullish, Support: $362.00, Resistance: $372.00

Lean Hogs (August '26) - Open: $96.90, Close: $97.10, +$0.20, Trend: Neutral, Support: $95.50, Resistance: $98.50

Supply Note: Daily cattle slaughter estimates held steady around 108,000 head. Packers are facing steep negative operational margins but simply lack the uncommitted physical inventory required to back-step cash prices, leaving northern feed yards securely in control of late-week procurement values.

Energies & Biofuels

WTI crude oil prices moved with a highly volatile, two-sided tone on Wednesday, coming under light structural pressure to trade just below the $69.00 level. The energy patch continues to sort through shifting geopolitical dynamics out of Doha, where diplomatic teams are attempting to salvage the fragile maritime peace framework. 

Despite the mild dip in the flat price, physical energy indicators remain exceptionally tight; the industry digested an implicit draw of over 6 million barrels in commercial crude stockpiles via the latest inventory tracking, marking the 11th consecutive weekly contraction in total domestic crude volumes and keeping product crack spreads defended.

Crude Oil (WTI) - August '26 - Open: $70.20, Close: $68.85, -$1.35, Trend: Bearish Tilt, Support: $67.50, Resistance: $71.00

Natural Gas - August '26 - Close: $3.21, -$0.01, Trend: Neutral/Bullish, Support: $3.10, Resistance: $3.35

Softs, Metals & Macros

The broader commodity space logged mixed performance as markets opened the third quarter of the year. In the softs ring, Canadian canola futures managed a slight positive finish, pulling minor support from the robust double-digit expansion across the U.S. soy complex. In the metals quadrant, gold and silver found a quiet stabilization floor, recovering from their worst quarterly performance in years as institutional capital cautiously realigned macro portfolios ahead of highly anticipated central bank labor and productivity updates.

Canola (November '26) - Close: $745.50, +$0.70, Trend: Neutral, Support: $735.00, Resistance: $755.00

Gold (August '26) - Close: $4,334.50, +$5.30, Trend: Neutral, Support: $4,280, Resistance: $4,380

Silver (September '26) - Close: $68.75, +$0.15, Trend: Neutral, Support: $67.50, Resistance: $70.00

The Pit Realignment

The technical landscape has officially flipped. The combination of tighter than expected old crop grain stocks and shrinking acreage profiles has fundamentally raised the price floor under the row crops. Performance tracking from today's open is solidly in the green for your grain positions, proving that the record short positions held by the fund community are still feeling the squeeze.

The Pollination Threat: Do not let your followers underestimate the shift in weather risk today. With the USDA corn stocks surprise eliminating 120 million bushels of projected carryout cushion, the market has zero room for an agronomic error. If the forecasted mid-July heat stress materializes across the Western Corn Belt during this early silking window, it will act as an immediate rocket underneath the September board.

Cash Cattle Discipline: The minor profit-taking pullback on the cattle board today means absolutely nothing to your cash bottom line. The fact that northern yards effortlessly batted away early $255.00 packer bids proves where the physical leverage sits. Keep your country asking prices firm the packers are critically short on uncommitted cattle and must come to the table before the week wraps up.

Energy Stock Tightness: While crude oil prices drifted lower due to macro asset reallocations, an 11th consecutive weekly draw in commercial crude inventories tells you the physical underlying pipeline is incredibly lean. This inventory vacuum should provide an ongoing structural backstop for bio-refining processing lines into late summer.

Tomorrow's Thoughts

Entering Thursday's session, the primary focus for the grain desks centers on whether the weather models confirm an intensifying dry pattern across the central I-states corridor, providing the technical clearance needed to challenge overhead resistance targets at $4.28 on September corn. In the livestock rings, look for cash cattle negotiations to break open in earnest. Armed with an expanding wholesale beef margin and exceptionally thin show lists, feed yards are well-positioned to squeeze additional premiums out of packers who cannot afford an empty hook.

US commercial crude inventories drew for a 10th straight week (-3.8mb) in the week through June 19th, totaling 57.4mb over that period and taking inventories to the least since September 2018, -10.6mb y/y.

quotedTweet.text:
New York: it's hot out there, and the power grid is working overtime to keep us cool.

Set your AC to 78 degrees, turn off lights/electronics you're not using, and unplug what you can.

Our City is doing its part too: maintaining the 78 degrees rule in our buildings, dimming/turning off our lights during peak electricity demand, asking private partners to do the same, and powering down non-essential equipment.

A stable grid means the AC stays on, and lives are saved. Let's ease demand — and get through the heat — together.

text:
Trump's move into Venezuela was never really about democracy.

It was about oil.

While the US was paying $60 to $70 a barrel, China was buying Venezuelan crude for around $20. Same story in Iran. Same story in Russia.

China didn't create the turmoil. It just showed up with a checkbook and bought the discount.

Venezuela at its peak produced several million barrels a day. That capacity didn't disappear. It atrophied under sanctions, mismanagement, and isolation. Chevron stayed. Everyone else left.

Now the majors are coming back. Service companies are returning. The infrastructure is being rebuilt.

But Venezuela is an OPEC member. Any production recovery happens inside a quota structure designed to manage global supply. More barrels from Caracas don't automatically mean lower prices at the pump.

Geopolitics and energy markets are not the same thing. Confusing them is expensive.
OPEC+ expected to boost oil output by 188,000 bpd in August, adding downward pressure on oil prices. Monitor weekend meeting.
OPEC+ may boost output by 188k bpd in August, oil prices await cooling. Nomura: chip stocks haven't peaked, data centers spending strong through 2027.
